title: Horizon Bias and the Term Structure of Equity Returns
abstract: We label the degree to which individuals are more optimistic at long horizons relative to short horizons as the horizon bias. We examine whether time-series variation in the horizon bias can explain the time-series variation in the equity term structure. We use analyst earnings forecasts to measure the degree of the horizon bias in the stock market. Consistent with the intuition from a stylized present value model, we find that periods of above-average horizon bias are associated with negative term premiums, whereas periods of below-average horizon bias are associated with positive term premiums.

article.1. Horizon Bias and the Term Structure o:
JEL G12 - Asset Pricing; Trading volume; Bond Interest Rates G41 - Role and Effects of Psychological, Emotional, Social, and Cognitive Factors on Decision Making in Financial Markets Issue Section: Article Editor: Stefano Giglio Stefano Giglio Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

A key ingredient in understanding the pricing of equity is understanding the term structure of equity returns.1A number of recent papers have examined the pricing of cash flows in the equity term structure. While scholars debate whether the term structure is upward sloping or downward sloping (van Binsbergen, Brandt, and Koijen 2012;Bansal et al. 2021), they generally agree on the significant time-series variation in the slope of the equity term structure (van Binsbergen et al. 2013;Gormsen 2021;Bansal et al. 2021). Existing explanations of the time-series variation in the equity term structure focus on the discount rate channel (Gormsen 2021;Goncalves 2021;Bansal et al. 2021).2We deviate from this approach by examining whether biased beliefs about cash flows are an important consideration. Specifically, we highlight the relevance of one bias, the optimism bias, for the term structure of equity returns.

The optimism bias suggests that people consider negative events less likely and positive events more likely than rational (Sharot 2011). An optimism bias in financial markets would suggest that investors expect firm growth rates to be higher than they will be, on average. In recent work,Cassella et al. (2021)explore how the optimism bias changes with the forecasting horizon. They find that the term-structure of optimism has significant time-series variation and is, on average, upward sloping (horizon bias). We explore whether time-series variation in the horizon bias explains time-series variation in the term structure of equity returns.

To highlight how horizon bias can affect the term structure of equity, we present a simple stylized present value model. To isolate the effect of beliefs about cash flows, we assume constant discount rates and let short- and long-horizon expected growth rates incorporate different degrees of optimism bias.3The model shows that the equity term premium is negatively related to the long-term growth forecast bias and positively related to the bias in forecasts of short-term cash flow growth rates. This simple stylized model illustrates how biased beliefs can explain time-series variation in the equity term structure.

To gauge the presence of horizon bias in the equity market, we use sell-side analysts’ earnings forecasts—a widely used measure of investor beliefs about firm fundamentals—to test the predictions of the model empirically. Assuming a random walk in earnings, we definehorizon biasas the difference between the growth rate forecasts and the long-term growth rate forecasts implied by short-term forecasts.4We predict that when the horizon bias is high, the term structure will be downward sloping, and when the horizon bias is low, the term structure will be upward sloping. This is precisely what we find: when the horizon bias is higher than its respective sample mean, the subsequent annual equity term premium is|$-$|8.04|$\%$|⁠, and it is 2.60|$\%$|otherwise. The slope of the term structure grows monotonically more negative as the horizon bias becomes more pronounced.

We hypothesize that our measured horizon bias reflects biased beliefs. However, horizon bias in sell-side analyst forecasts could also reflect analyst incentives. A stream of the accounting literature notes a walkdown in earnings (i.e., forecasts trend downward as the announcement date moves closer). This is often attributed to the incentives of sell-side analysts to curry favor with management (Kang, O’Brien, and Sivaramakrishnan 1994;Richardson, Teoh, and Wysocki 2004;Raedy, Shane, and Yang 2006). Therefore, it is possible that our measure of the horizon bias is contaminated by analyst incentives. One way to address this concern is to construct a sample that is less affected by these incentives. We repeat our analysis for unaffiliated analysts, that is, analysts not tied to investment banks, since they are less likely to distort their forecasts to curry favor with management (Lin and McNichols 1998;O’Brien, McNichols, and Hsiou-Wei 2005;Corwin, Larocque, and Stegemoller 2017).5Using this sample to construct our measure, we find that the horizon bias has stronger explanatory power. Specifically, when the horizon bias is higher than its respective mean, the subsequent annual equity term premium is|$-$|11.87|$\%$|⁠, and it is 4.72|$\%$|otherwise.

These results indicate that the equity term premium is high when the horizon bias is low, and the equity term premium is low when the horizon bias is high. We extend these results to see whether the horizon bias can predict the realized equity term premium. We regress the realized equity term premium over the next year on the current value of the horizon bias. We find that a one-standard-deviation increase in the horizon bias predicts an equity term premium that is 10.5|$\%$|lower (⁠|$t$|-stat.|$= -2.09$|⁠). The results are stronger when we exclude forecasts of affiliated analysts. The predictive relation is highly significant and holds for alternative horizons ranging from 6 to 60 months after controlling for several predictors previously shown to predict equity premiums.

While we try our best to address measurement error, it is still likely that our estimate of the horizon bias in cash flow expectations is noisy. The beliefs of sell-side analysts may not reflect the beliefs of the investor population. Theoretical work fromCassella et al. (2021), which builds on motivated belief frameworks fromBrunnermeier and Parker (2005)andCaplin and Leahy (2019), suggests that the horizon bias should be stronger when long-term skewness and uncertainty about the distant future are high relative to short-term skewness and uncertainty, respectively. As skewness increases, the true expected (nonanticipatory) utility cost of holding optimistic beliefs decreases. In other words, optimism increases as the asset becomes more positively skewed because it is cheap to buy consumption in the low probability but very positive outcome states. Furthermore, higher uncertainty about the future makes it easier for one to manipulate their beliefs. This work suggests that if the measured horizon bias is high, and either long-term skewness is high relative to short-term skewness or long-term uncertainty is high relative to short-term uncertainty, then our measured horizon bias more likely reflects high investor optimism at long-horizons relative to short-horizons. If neither horizon-differenced measure is high, we are more likely to be capturing noise with our measure. Therefore, an interaction between a horizon-differenced skewness or a horizon-differenced uncertainty and our measure of the horizon bias should better explain the term structure of equity returns.

We approximate the skewness of the earnings distribution by applying quantile regressions to earnings data. We construct an uncertainty measure using the methodology ofJurado, Ludvigson, and Ng (2015)applied to earnings. We find strong support for our hypothesis: The annual equity term premium is|$-$|7.65|$\%$|when our interaction of horizon bias and horizon-differencedSkewnessmeasure is above the mean. In contrast, the annual equity term premium is 1|$\%$|when our interaction of horizon bias and horizon-differencedSkewnessmeasure is below the mean. Similarly, we see an annual equity term premium equal to -17.76|$\%$|lower when our interaction of horizon bias and horizon-differencedUncertaintymeasure is above the mean. In contrast, we see an annual equity term premium of 3.2|$\%$|when the interaction of horizon bias and our horizon-differencedUncertaintymeasure is below the mean. We find similar evidence in predictive regressions.

Another potential concern is related to the assumption that long- and short-term forecasts follow a random walk. With a different data generating process, we could be capturing rational differences in forecasts at long versus short horizons. To alleviate this concern, we use a random forest methodology and the information set proposed byvan Binsbergen, Han, and Lopez-Lira (2022)to generate a proxy for “rational” earnings forecasts. As our measure of bias, we use the difference between analyst forecasts and these “rational” forecasts. We find that horizon-differenced forecasts based on the bias are related to the equity term premium, while those based on “rational” forecasts are not.

Our paper contributes to the literature on the term structure of equity returns. We extend the time series of the equity term structure ofvan Binsbergen, Brandt, and Koijen (2012)to more recent years and provide a new explanation for its time-series variation.6Most related to our work areGormsen (2021),Bansal et al. 2021, andGoncalves 2021.Gormsen (2021)argues that, to capture both the level and cyclicality in the equity term structure, asset pricing models need to incorporate at least two priced risk factors.Bansal et al. 2021estimate a Bayesian regime-switching model to study the time-series variation in the equity term premium during a sample from 2004 to 2017. They argue that an equilibrium regime-switching consumption-based long-run risk model can reproduce the conditional and unconditional empirical findings about the equity term structure slope.Goncalves 2021argues that the empirical patterns in the equity term structure can be reconciled within the intertemporal capital asset pricing model (ICAPM), provided that the risk aversion coefficient is relatively high. We differ from these papers in that we tie the slope of the equity term structure to biased beliefs about fundamentals and document a close link between the term structure of equity and the term structure of optimism bias.

Regarding cash flow expectations, the papers most related to ours areDa and Warachka (2011)andWeber (2018).Da and Warachka (2011)show that the difference between long-term and short-term earnings forecasts is a robust predictor of returns in the cross-section of equity returns.Weber (2018)relates differences in cash flow beliefs to the cross-section of long- and short-duration stocks. We differ in that we consider the aggregate stock market and offer motivated beliefs as the underlying mechanism for biased beliefs.

More broadly, we contribute to the large literature on what drives equity prices - discount rate news or cash flow news (Campbell and Shiller 1988;van Binsbergen and Koijen 2010;Cochrane 2011;Golez and Koudijs 2018).De la O and Myers (2021)andChen, Da, and Zhao (2013)find a strong association between cash flow expectations and asset prices. In particular,De la O and Myers (2021)show that the connection between cash flow expectations and asset prices is stronger than the connection between expected returns and asset prices. We add to this growing literature by relating short (long)-term cash flow expectations to assets with a short (long) duration. In this sense, our findings can be seen as a more stringent test of the importance of cash flow beliefs.

Finally, we add to a growing literature that explores the impact of motivated beliefs on financial markets (Brunnermeier and Parker 2005;Caplin and Leahy 2019;Banerjee, Davis, and Gondhi 2021). To the best of our knowledge, our paper is the first to offer evidence on the role of motivated beliefs for the term structure of stock returns.
The price of an asset can be defined as $$\begin{align}
P = \frac{E[\mbox{Future cash flows]}}{\mbox{Discount rate}}.
\end{align}$$ (1)

This suggests that the price of an asset can change with changes in expected future cash flows and/or changes in the discount rate. Both quantities can vary over time and across horizons. In the simple model below, we assume a constant discount rate, and we only allow expected cash flows to vary across the horizon The expected cash flow can also differ from the actual cash flow. This setup provides us with a simple setting to illustrate how biased beliefs about cash flows can affect asset prices.

We recognize that in realitybothdiscount rates and expected future cash flows are important drivers of asset prices. In our empirical analysis, we will control for discount rates to more cleanly study the belief-based channel.

To derive the implications for the term structure of equity returns, we assume two assets. The short-term ( ST ) asset entitles the owner to a dividend |$D_{t+1} $| in the next period. The long-term ( LT ) asset entitles the owner to an infinite stream of future dividends. Using the present-value relation, we can write the prices of the short-term asset and the long-term asset as follows: $$\begin{align}
P_t^{\it ST} &= \frac{D_t (1+g^{\it ST})}{(1+r)},\\
\end{align}$$ (2) $$\begin{align}
P_t^{\it LT} &= \frac{D_t (1+g^{\it ST})}{(1+r)}+\frac{1}{(1+r)}\frac{D_t (1
+ g^{\it ST})(1+g^{\it LT})}{(r - g^{\it LT})},
\end{align}$$ (3) where |$r$| is a constant required rate of return, |$D_t $| is the dividend observed at the end of the current period, |$g^{\it ST}$| is investors’ forecast of dividend growth rate in the next period, and |$g^{\it LT}$| is investors’ forecast of dividend growth in every period after that. Suppose that the actual dividend growth rate is |$g^R$|⁠ . Then, the next-period price of the long-term asset and the next-period dividend are $$\begin{align}
P_{t+1}^{\it LT} &= P_t^{\it LT} (1+g^R),\\
\end{align}$$ (4) $$\begin{align}D_{t+1} &= D_t (1+g^R).\end{align}$$ (5)

Since the short-term asset has no claim to any future dividends, the next-period price of the short-term asset is, by construction, zero, that is,|$P_{t+1}^{\it ST} = 0$|⁠.

The equity term premium is the difference between the expected return on the long-term asset and the expected return on the short-term asset. Based on the above setup, we can express the next-period equity term premium |${\it ETP}_{t+1} $|⁠ , or difference in expected returns between long-term asset and short-term asset, as (Appendix A.1 provides the details): $$\begin{align}
{\it ETP}_{t+1} = \mbox{E}\left[ {R_{t+1}^{\it LT} - R_{t+1}^{\it ST} } \right] = -(g^{\it LT} - g^{\it ST})\left( {\frac{1+g^R}{1+g^{\it ST}}} \right)\!.\end{align}$$ (6)

The main takeaway from Equation ( 6 ) is that the equity term premium is negatively associated with the stock market participants’ forecasted difference in the long-term and short-term growth rates. That is, when investors’ forecast of long-term growth is more optimistic than investors’ forecast of short-term growth, the subsequent equity term premium is negative. With a constant actual growth rate in fundamentals |$g^R$| over all horizons, it also follows that $$\begin{align}
{\it ETP} \propto - \left( {{\it Bias}^{\it LT} - {\it Bias}^{\it ST}} \right),
\end{align}$$ (7) where |${\it Bias}^{\it LT} ({\it Bias}^{\it ST})$| is the difference between investors’ long-term (short-term) forecast and the actual dividend growth rate, |$g^R$|⁠ . In other words, when the bias in long-term growth is larger than the bias in short-term growth, the long-term asset is overpriced more than the short-term asset, and such overpricing leads to a negative equity term premium.

Note that the short-term growth rate affects the prices of both short- and long-term assets, whereas the long-term growth rate affects only the price of the long-term asset. This suggests that the relative overpricing of long-versus short-term assets merely depends on the bias in the long-term growth rate. Formally, we can show this by taking a ratio of the price-to-dividend ratios for the long-term asset and the short-term asset: $$\begin{align}
\left( {\frac{P_t^{\it LT} / D_t }{P_t^{\it ST} / D_t }} \right) = 1+\frac{(1+
g^{\it LT})}{(r - g^{\it LT})}.\end{align}$$ (8)

This leads to the second prediction of the model: a larger bias in the long-term growth rate implies a larger value of the long-term price-to-dividend ratio compared to the short-term price-to-dividend ratio.

The model helps us understand how biased beliefs can explain time-series variation in the equity term structure. The model, however, is very stylized. We assume a constant discount rate across maturities, which is a large simplification. We do this merely to illustrate the economic mechanism we have in mind.

As mentioned above, we control for variables related to discount rate variation in the empirical section. Controlling for discount rate variation is important to properly attribute variation in asset prices to variation in expected cash flows (Bordalo et al. 2020;Giglio et al. 2021). Since expected returns tend to be positively correlated with cash flow expectations (e.g.,Lettau and Ludvigson 2005;van Binsbergen and Koijen 2010;Giglio et al. 2021), then, without control variables, we may be underestimating the effect of cash flow expectations for explaining time-series variation in the term structure of equity returns. This is because the slope of optimism bias is negatively related to the realized equity term premium and the omitted variable bias predicts that the estimated coefficient is upward biased when the missing variable is positively correlated with the treated variable.
article.2. Data and Empirical Methodology:
In this section, we describe how we calculate various measures used in the paper. We end the section with a discussion of summary statistics.

2.1 Equity term structure

We measure the slope of the equity term structure as in (van Binsbergen, Brandt, and Koijen, 2012, henceforth BBK) by comparing returns on the S&P 500 index and returns on a short-term dividend strip. The monthly return on the index consists of monthly dividends and capital appreciation of the index. Similarly, the monthly return on the dividend strip strategy consists of monthly dividends plus the change in the price of the dividend strip. Maturities of dividend strips range from 1.9 to 1.3 years, with rebalancing occurring every January and July. Details are provided in BBK’sinternet appendix. We obtain BBK’s data from 1996 to October 2009 from their website and then extend their data to December 2017. We carefully follow their empirical approach.

In particular, we obtain the SPX minute-by-minute options and index data from the CBOE for the period between January 2004 and December 2017. We retain only standard SPX options with expirations on the third Friday in a month, and we exclude options with the bid or ask prices below $3. As in BBK, we use observations between 10 a.m. and 2 p.m. on the last trading day in a month. For the risk-free rate, we use the Zero-Curve rate from OptionMetrics. We linearly interpolate among the given zero-curve maturities to match the maturity of the options. Using the bid-ask midpoint, we calculate the price of a dividend strip for all the put-call pairs with the same strike price and maturity. At each end of month, we take the median across all the dividend strip prices for a given maturity. Finally, we calculate returns on a dividend strategy. As in BBK, we rely on maturities between 1.9 and 1.3 years, with rebalancing in January and July. The only exception is July 2013 to January 2014, during which we let the strategy rely on maturities between 1.5 and 0.9 years because the appropriate maturity options (expiration in June 2015) were not listed until September 2013. As inGolez (2014), we calculate daily realized dividends from the Datastream S&P 500 return index and total return index. For the overlapping period January 2004 to October 2009, returns on our dividend strategy have a correlation of 0.98 with returns on the same series provided by BBK. For the final time series, running from January 1996 until December 2017, we append our data to BBK’s data in October 2009.

One of the critiques of BBK is that their results may be sensitive to the proxy for the interest rates used in the calculation of dividend strip prices (Song 2016). Therefore, we additionally obtain the dividend strip price data fromGolez and Jackwerth (2022). They estimate dividend strip prices in the same way as BBK, except that they use the interest rate implied by option prices (van Binsbergen, Diamond, and Grotteria 2022).

2.2 Short-term and long-term earnings growth forecasts

We use data from Institutional Brokers’ Estimate System (IBES) unadjusted summary statistics to determine analysts’ estimates of short-term and long-term earnings growth. We use a bottom-up approach and start with forecasts from IBES at the firm level. We restrict our sample to firms in the S&P 500. We examine this year’s earnings forecast, next year’s earnings forecast, and analysts’ estimates of long-term growth. We drop observations that are missing one of these forecasts or missing the previous year’s actual earnings. We drop observations for which the consensus records (or stated quarter ends) are one day before, on, or one day after the earnings announcement day. A firm’s fiscal year can end at various points in the calendar year, so annual earnings forecasts will not be at the same horizon for all firms. To standardize the forecasting horizon across all firms, we construct a 1-year earnings forecast based on interpolating a forecast of this year’s annual earnings and a forecast of next year’s annual earnings. Specifically, we use the fraction of a year between the forecast date and the predicted next annual earnings announcement date—1 year from the previous earnings announcement date—as a weight on this year’s earnings forecast. For example, with exactly 6 months until the predicted earnings announcement date, this year’s forecast and next year’s forecast would be equally weighted in generating a 1-year earnings forecast. We merge this information with CRSP by ticker using a CRSP/IBES linking table. To arrive at marketwide estimates of future earnings, we first multiply earnings per share forecasts by the number of shares from the associated month and then aggregate forecasts for this year/next year. We divide these forecasts by lagged earnings for the same set of firms to arrive at an estimate for short-term earnings growth.7To arrive at an estimate for long-term earnings growth for the aggregate stock market, we calculate the market value-weighted average long-term growth estimate for the same set of firms.

Our horizon bias measure captures the difference between the long-term and short-term growth rates. In the model, the equity term premium is inversely proportional to the difference in the long-term and short-term growth rate bias (Equation ( 6 )). Assuming a random walk for the rational growth rate in fundamentals, this is proportional to the difference between the long-term and short-term growth rates. We use a random walk as our first measure of a rational forecast for its simplicity and documented accruacy (e.g., Bradshaw et al. 2012 ; Easton et al. 2022 ). 8 In practice, the difference between the growth rates is dominated by the much larger volatility of the short-term growth rate. To ensure comparability between the growth rates, we assume an AR(1) process for the short-term growth rate. We then calculate long-term growth implied by the short-term growth rate (Appendix A.2 provides the details): $$\begin{align}
{\it LTG}_t^{Implied} = \frac{1}{5}\left[ {4\phi _0+3\phi _0 \phi _1+
2\phi _0 \phi _1^2+\phi _0 \phi _1^3 + {\it STG}_t \left( {1+
\phi _1+\phi _1^2+\phi _1^3+\phi _1^4 } \right)} \right]\!,\end{align}$$ (9) where |$\phi _0 $| is the mean for the short-term growth rate and |$\phi_1 $| is the annual persistence. We estimate the autoregressive process for the short-term growth rate at the monthly frequency. We take the AR( 12 ) coefficient as our measure for the annual persistence. Finally, we define the horizon bias as the difference between the long-term growth rate and the long-term growth rate implied by the short-term growth rate: 9 $$\begin{align}
{\it HB}_t = {\it LTG}_t - {\it LTG}_t^{\it Implied}.\end{align}$$ (10)

2.3 Affiliated analyst identification

We identify affiliated analysts using a number of different data sets. We thank John Loudis for sharing a data set that connects broker identities (as listed in IBES) with banks from the SDC database since 1999. Broker identities in IBES are no longer reliable for data sets downloaded from WRDS. We thank Jessie Watkins for providing an IBES data set downloaded in 2015. These data sets allow us to tie analysts to specific banks over the period 1999–2015. Following the literature, we identify an analyst as affiliated with a firm if their associated bank was connected to the firm’s initial public offering (IPO) in the past 5 years, the firm’s seasoned equity offering (SEO) within the past 2 years, or was the lead underwriter on bond issuance in the past year. In total, around 35|$\%$|of forecasts classify as those coming from affiliated analysts.

2.4 Skewness

We want to proxy for the difference between long-horizon skewness and short-horizon skewness. We can approximate how skewed the earnings distribution is by forecasting different quantiles of the earnings distribution. If the absolute difference between high percentile forecasts and the median forecast is very large (small) relative to the absolute difference between low percentile forecasts and the median forecast, it is likely that the distribution is positively (negatively) skewed. We predict different quantiles of the earnings distribution by applying quantile regressions to the earnings prediction model of Hou, Van Dijk, and Zhang (2012) . 10 We aggregate 95th percentile earnings forecasts, 50th percentile earnings forecasts, and 5th percentile earnings forecasts to create aggregate market earnings forecasts for different parts of the earnings distribution. We proxy for aggregate market earnings skewness by calculating: $$\begin{align}
&\text{Skewness}_t\nonumber\\[-3pt]
& \ \ =\left(\!\!\frac{\text{95th Percentile Earnings
Forecast}_t- \text{50th Percentile Earnings
Forecast}_t}{\text{50th Percentile Earnings
Forecast}_t- \text{5th Percentile Earnings Forecast}_t}\!\!\!\right)\!.\end{align}$$ (11)

We are interested in the difference between long-term skewness and short-term skewness. Therefore, we calculate ourSkewnessmeasure separately based on 1- and 5-year earnings forecasts. We normalize both skewness measures to have a mean of zero and standard deviation of one. Finally, we take the difference between 5- and 1-year skewness.

2.5 Uncertainty

Jurado, Ludvigson, and Ng (2015) formalize uncertainty as the conditional volatility of the unforecastable component of future values in the series. We apply this methodology to generate a measure of long-term earnings uncertainty and short-term earnings uncertainty. Specifically, we calculate earnings uncertainty for |$h$| -years ahead as $$\begin{align}
U_t (h) = \sqrt {E[(y_{t+h} - E[y_{t+h} \vert I_t ])^2\vert I_t ]}.
\end{align}$$ (12)

Earnings is represented by|$y$|⁠, and the information set is represented by|$I$|⁠. We use random forests and the information set proposed byvan Binsbergen, Han, and Lopez-Lira (2022)to generate earnings forecasts.11We form expectations of squared forecast errors using the same information set and, again, using random forest regressions. The square root of this expectation serves as|$h$|-period-ahead uncertainty. We normalize our uncertainty measures to be centered on zero and have a standard deviation of one. We are interested in the difference between long-term uncertainty and short-term uncertainty. Therefore, we take the difference between long-term uncertainty and short-term uncertainty|$(U_t (5) - U_t (1))$|to generate ourUncertaintymeasure.

2.6 Other control variables

We collect data for several controls for state variables from traditional asset pricing models. We control for the P/D ratio of the market (Campbell and Shiller 1988) and the P/D ratio of dividend strips (van Binsbergen, Brandt, and Koijen 2012). We also consider other controls for countercyclical risk premiums, such asLettau and Ludvigson’s (2001) consumption to wealth-ratio, CAY,Fama and French’s (1989) measures of bond default and term spreads, and a dummy for NBER recessionary periods.

The identification of NBER recessions comes from the NBER website. The P/D ratio for the market is the current level of the S&P 500 index divided by the sum of dividends over the past year. The P/D ratio for the dividend strip is the current price of the 18-month dividend strip divided by the sum of dividends over the past year.

In our model, the difference between the price-dividend ratios is fully explained by the difference in long-term and short-term cash flow expectations. In reality, price-dividend ratios vary also because of changes in discount rates. Therefore, we also define the “error” term as the residual in the explanatory regression of the difference between the logarithm of price-to-dividend ratios on the horizon bias: $$\begin{align}
pd_t^{Mkt} - pd_t^{Strip} = \alpha+\beta {\it HB}_t+\varepsilon _t.\end{align}$$ (13)

The “error” term|$\varepsilon$|captures the difference in the price-dividend ratios that is not explained by the difference in the cash flow expectations, and thus we use it as a proxy for the term structure of discount rates.

We obtain data on the term structure of Treasury bonds and the yield spread between junk and investment-grade corporate bonds from Amit Goyal.Lettau and Ludvigson’s (2001) CAY is from Martin Lettau’s website.

2.7 Summary statistics

Tables 1and2report the summary statistics and the correlation structure for monthly data. The time period for the equity term premium estimates spans February 1996 through December 2017. All other variables start in January 1996 and run until December 2016. To minimize volatility bias in mean returns caused by measurement errors, we rely on logarithmic returns (Boguth et al. 2019).

article.3. Horizon Bias and the Equity Term Prem:
In this section, we test the economic intuition highlighted in our simple present value model that long-term prices are high relative to short-term prices when long-term growth forecasts are high relative to short-term growth forecasts. First, we document that the value of the aggregate market relative to the value of the associated dividend strip strongly correlates with the horizon bias. Next, we show that the equity term premium varies substantially across the states with low and high horizon bias. Finally, we recalculate horizon bias by dropping forecasts from affiliated analysts and show that this measure is even more closely related to the equity term structure.

3.1 Horizon bias and P/D ratios

Our present value model shows that, if expectations regarding cash flows matter for equity prices, the relative price of long- versus short-term assets is associated with the expected long-term growth rate. We find that the difference in the logarithm of the price-to-dividend ratio for the market and the logarithm of the price-to-dividend ratio for the dividend strip is highly correlated with the logarithm of the long-term growth rate. Specifically, we find that this difference has a correlation of 0.87 with the long-term growth rate and about 0.79 with the horizon bias. This suggests that the horizon bias explains about 63|$\%$|of the variation in the relative prices of the long-term and short-term assets. Overall, this indicates that beliefs about fundamentals are of first-order importance for asset prices (see alsoChen, Da, and Zhao 2013;De la O and Myers (2021)).

3.2 Horizon bias and equity term premium

Next, we turn to our main prediction that the horizon bias is negatively related to the equity term premium. We focus on the equity term premium over the next 12 months to provide ample time for price correction and to minimize the effect of measurement errors.Figure 4plots the horizon bias along with the future annual realized term premium. The figure is suggestive of a negative relation between the horizon bias and the future realized term premium. The negative relation is most apparent around the Nasdaq bubble, when long-term growth rate forecasts peaked and ex post realized term premium was particularly low.12

When the horizon bias is above its mean, the ex post realized term premium is|$-$|8.04|$\%$|⁠. When the horizon bias is below its mean, the ex post realized term premium is 2.6|$\%$|⁠.

3.3 Affiliated analysts, horizon bias, and equity term premium

Analyst forecasts may be biased by incentives to appeal to management. This will hamper our measure from capturing the “true” horizon bias. We follow the existing literature to mitigate the role of incentives in our measure. Specifically, the literature has established that the incentive to appeal to management is particularly strong for affiliated analysts (Kang, O’Brien, and Sivaramakrishnan 1994;Richardson, Teoh, and Wysocki 2004;Raedy, Shane, and Yang 2006). To understand the extent to which our findings are driven by analysts’ “incentives” rather than horizon bias, we drop affiliated analyst forecasts for the period 1999–2015 and repeat our analysis from Section3.2.

We report results from this new analysis in the second column inTable 3. We find stronger evidence that a positive horizon bias is associated with a negative equity term premium and a negative horizon bias is associated with a positive equity term premium. When horizon bias is above its mean, the ex post realized term premium is|$-$|11.87|$\%$|⁠. When horizon bias is below its mean, the ex post realized term premium is 4.72|$\%$|⁠.
article.4. Predictive Regressions:
In this section, we show the results from predictive regressions. Specifically, we test whether the horizon bias can predict the difference between the 1-year realized return on the S&P 500 and dividend strips, which we call the realized equity term premium.

4.1 Baseline predictive regressions

We first test whether the horizon bias, as measured among all analysts, can predict the realized equity term premium over the next year. We present the results inTable 4. We use Newey-West|$t$|-statistics with a lag of 11 to account for the overlapping nature of the data. We find that a larger horizon bias predicts lower realized equity term premiums. Specifically, in column 1, we see that a one-standard-deviation increase in the horizon bias predicts a realized equity term premium that is 10.5|$\%$|smaller (⁠|$t$|-stat.|$= -2.09$|⁠). Since prices are driven by changes in cash flows and changes in discount rates, it is important to control for discount rates. In the second column, we add controls related to discount rates. Specifically, we include the S&P 500 P/D ratio, the bond term spread, the default spread, and CAY. After including these controls, we still find that a larger horizon bias predicts lower realized equity term premiums. Specifically, a one-standard-deviation increase in the horizon bias predicts a realized equity term premium that is 11.9 percentage points lower. In the third column, we include theErrorterm. This control captures the difference between price-dividend ratios for long- and short-duration assets that is orthogonal to the horizon bias, and we, therefore, use it as a proxy for the term structure of discount rates. After including this control, we find even stronger results. A one-standard-deviation increase in the horizon bias predicts a realized equity term premium that is 20.5-percentage-points lower (⁠|$t$|-stat.|$= -5.89$|⁠). The increase in the economic magnitude of horizon bias is consistent with the positive correlation of cash flow expectations and discount rates (Lettau and Ludvigson 2005;van Binsbergen and Koijen 2010;Giglio et al. 2021). Given such positive correlation and the fact that horizon bias is negatively related to future term premium, the standard omitted variable bias suggests that controlling for the discount rate channel should increase the economic magnitude of cash flow forecasts. In the last column, we add a control for NBER recessions. We find similar, albeit slightly weaker, results.13On the right side ofTable 4, we present results after dropping affiliated analyst forecasts for the period 1999–2015. After dropping affiliated analyst forecasts, we find that the horizon bias still strongly predicts negative realized equity term premiums.14

4.2 The term structure of equity and motivated beliefs

While we do our best to address measurement error in horizon bias, analysts’ forecasts may not reflect the beliefs of all investors in the market. In addition, we have implicitly assumed that rational forecasts would generate the same growth rate for short-term and long-term forecasts. In their theoretical analysis,Cassella et al. (2021)predict when the horizon bias should be stronger. Specifically, their analysis suggests that horizon bias should be higher when forecasts of long-term skewness and uncertainty are high relative to forecasts of short-term skewness and uncertainty, respectively. Skewness is associated with more optimism since it is cheap to buy consumption in the low probability but very positive outcome states. This reduces the expected utility cost of motivated beliefs since these belief distortions do not result in significant consumption distortions in other states of the world. Uncertainty is associated with more optimism since it is less costly to manipulate beliefs during periods of higher uncertainty.

To the extent that horizon bias is driven by motivated beliefs, the above arguments suggest that we are more likely capturing the underlying difference in optimism between long-horizons and short-horizons with our horizon bias measure when the skewness and uncertainty of the long-term earnings distribution is high relative to the skewness and uncertainty of the short-term earnings distribution.

In other words, ourmeasuredhorizon bias should be associated with a more negative realized equity term premium when long-term skewness (uncertainty) is high relative to short-term skewness (uncertainty). We test this by interacting the horizon-differenced skewness (horizon-differenced uncertainty) with our measured horizon bias and connecting it to realized equity term premiums.

We present the results inTable 6. When the horizon bias and horizon-differenced skewness interaction term is above (below) the mean, the annual equity term premium is about|$-$|7.65|$\%$|(1.0|$\%$|⁠). When the horizon bias and horizon-differenced uncertainty interaction term is above (below) the mean, the annual equity term premium is about|$-$|17.76|$\%$|(3.2|$\%$|⁠).

article.5. Rational Forecasts Generated from Mac:
Thus far, when calculating the horizon bias, we have implicitly assumed that rational forecasts of annualized earnings growth are the same for long-term forecasts and short-term forecasts. Therefore, the difference between long-term forecasts and long-term growth forecasts implied by short-term growth forecasts should give us a measure of the horizon bias. In this section, we repeat our analysis allowing for long-term and short-term rational growth forecasts to be different. Specifically, we generate “rational” earnings forecasts using a random forest methodology and the information set proposed byvan Binsbergen, Han, and Lopez-Lira (2022). The random forest methodology is a machine learning technique that can be used to solve regression and classification problems. It aggregates the results of decision trees and injects randomness into how the decision trees are constructed. Decision trees start with a training data set and look across predictors and split values to find a partition that minimizes the sum of squared errors in the two new regions. The process is then repeated for the resultant regions, and this process continues until some stopping criteria is reached. We use random forest regressions to predict earnings. The information set used or variables considered byvan Binsbergen, Han, and Lopez-Lira (2022)can be broken into three categories: (1) firm fundamentals, (2) macroeconomic conditions, and (3) sell-side analyst forecasts. We describe the procedure and information set used in full detail in the AppendixA.3.1.

Using this procedure, we generate long-term and short-term “rational” earnings growth forecasts. “Bias” is defined as actual growth rates minus the rational component: $$\begin{align}
\begin{aligned}
{\it LTG}^{\it Bias} &= {\it LTG} - {\it LTG}^{\it Rational} \\
{\it STG}^{\it Bias} &= {\it STG} - {\it STG}^{\it Rational} \\
\end{aligned}.
\end{align}$$ (15)

This decomposition enables us to differentiate between the “rational” and the “bias” component of the horizon-differenced forecasts. That is, the “rational” horizon-differenced forecast is the difference between the “rational” long-term earnings growth forecast and the long-term growth forecast implied by a “rational” short-term earnings growth rate. Similarly, “bias” is the difference between the “bias” in the long-term earnings growth forecast and the long-term growth forecast implied by a “bias” in the short-term earnings growth rate.

We present the results graphically inFigure 7. We do not find any clear relationship between the rational component of differences in cash flow forecasts and the equity term premiums (panel B). In contrast, when looking at the biased component of forecasts in panel A ofFigure 7, we see a clear inverse relationship between the differences in growth forecasts and the equity term structure. We also run predictive regressions of the equity term premium on each component of the horizon-differenced forecasts, while controlling for variables that capture discount rate variation. Again, it is important to control for discount rate variation since prices are driven by not only cash flow forecasts but also discount rates.Table 8reports the results. The estimated coefficient for the biased component of the horizon-differenced forecasts is negative and significant. The estimated coefficient for the rational component of horizon-differenced forecasts is close to zero and insignificant.

6.1 Subsample analysis

Columns 1 and 2 report the results separately for the first half of the sample period and the second half of the sample period. The estimated coefficient for the horizon bias is more negative in the first half of the sample, but it is strongly significant in both periods.

6.2 Use of options data to calculate the equity term premium

In the main analysis, we use dividend strips estimated from S&P 500 options fromvan Binsbergen, Brandt, and Koijen (2012). We follow their methodology to extend the data until the end of 2017. A potential concern is that dividend strip prices depend on the assumed risk-free rate (Song 2016). To address this concern, we obtain data fromGolez and Jackwerth (2022), who reestimate BBK dividend strip prices using the endogenous interest rate implied by option prices. Column 3 reports the results. The estimated coefficient for the horizon bias remains negative and strongly significant.

Another potential concern is that the results depend on the maturity of dividend strips. For the period that we have our own options data (2004 onward), we have recalculated dividend strip returns using 0.9-year maturity dividend strip instead of the 1.9-year maturity strip. Column 4 reports the results. The estimated coefficient for the horizon bias is somewhat smaller (consistent with the above subsample analysis showing that the coefficient is lower in the second half of the sample) but remains statistically significant.

6.3 Measuring horizon bias

We defined horizon bias as the difference between the long-term growth rate and the long-term growth rate implied from the short-term growth rate (Equation (9)). A potential concern is that the results depend on the AR(1) transformation that we use to calculate implied long-term growth rate. We now alternatively measure horizon bias as the difference between the standardized long-term growth rate and the standardized short-term growth rate. Column 5 reports the results. We find that results remain strong and significant.

Another concern may be that the reporting of long-term forecasts differs from the fiscal-year earnings forecasts that we use to create short-term forecasts. In column 6, we repeat results using horizon bias based on the difference between 4- and 1-year forecasts, where both are based on fiscal year earnings forecasts. Because of low data coverage of longer-term fiscal year forecasts, we had to replace two missing observations (January 1997 and February 2000) with observations from the previous months. Despite these data issues, the results are robust, as we find a negative and significant coefficient for the horizon bias.

6.4 Dividends

In our theoretical setup, prices depend on dividends, whereas empirically, we measure horizon bias using analysts’ earnings forecasts. We use analysts’ earnings forecasts because they cover a longer time period (dividend forecasts start in 2002) and have better coverage than dividend forecasts. The concern is that the short-term earnings, in particular, are more volatile than dividend forecasts. We consider two alternatives for estimating short-term cash flows. First, we useLintner (1956)model to relate earnings to dividends. We estimate the model on data from 1946 until 1995. We then use the estimated parameters to transform annual earnings forecasts to dividend forecasts in our sample period. We recalculate horizon bias and report results in column 7. The estimated coefficient for horizon bias is negative and significant. Next, we collect the data on analysts’ dividend forecasts. We follow the same estimation procedure as for earnings to estimate short-term aggregate dividend forecasts from 2003 onward. We then replace earnings forecasts with dividend forecasts for that period and recalculate the horizon bias. Column 8 reports the results. The estimated coefficient for horizon bias is a bit smaller but still economically important and statistically significant.

6.5 Extrapolative beliefs

We propose motivated beliefs as the potential channel driving biased beliefs. Biased beliefs also could be driven by cash flow extrapolation (Weber 2018). We construct a proxy for extrapolation using past earnings growth. Presumably, investors extrapolate short-horizon growth rates by looking at trends in most recent growth rates, and they extrapolate longer-term growth rates by using information from a longer lookback window. Accordingly, we use lagged annual growth rate as a measure of short-term extrapolation and a 5-year average of earnings growth rates as a measure of longer-term extrapolation. In column 9, we add the difference between these growth rates as the control for cash flow extrapolation in the predictive regression for future equity term premium. The estimated coefficient for the horizon bias hardly changes and remains significant.
article.7. Conclusion:
We document a strong and robust relationship between the horizon bias, or the difference in optimism at long versus short horizons, and equity term premiums. Consistent with the economic intuition from a simple stylized model, periods of above-average horizon bias have lower equity term premiums and periods of below-average horizon bias have higher equity term premiums. We then show that the horizon bias predicts realized equity term premiums after including discount rate controls.

Recent work suggests that the horizon bias is driven by motivated reasoning, which predicts that the horizon bias should increase when the difference between long-term and short-term skewness and the difference between long-term and short-term uncertainty is high. We test this prediction and find that the equity term premium is lower when both skewness/uncertainty and themeasuredhorizon bias are high. We also offer evidence that our results cannot easily be reconciled with differences in “rational” cash flow forecasts at short and long horizons. Specifically, we show that the equity term premium is not related to differences in earnings forecasts generated via random forests but is related to the bias component of earnings forecasts. Overall, we provide evidence that biased beliefs are an important component toward understanding the puzzling patterns in the equity term structure.

A.1 Term Structure of Equity

We consider two risky assets. The first is a short-term ( ST ) asset, since its owner is entitled to receive a liquidating dividend, |$D_{t+1}$|⁠ , in the next period. The second asset is a long-term ( LT ) asset, which entitles the owner to an infinite sequence of future dividends. Using the present-value relation, one can write the prices of the short-term asset and the long-term asset as follows: $$\begin{align}
P_t^{\it ST} &= \frac{D_t (1+g^{\it ST})}{(1+r)},\\
\end{align}$$ (A.1) $$\begin{align}
P_t^{\it LT} &= \frac{D_t (1+g^{\it ST})}{(1+r)}+\frac{1}{(1+r)}\frac{D_t (1
+ g^{\it ST})(1+g^{\it LT})}{(r - g^{\it LT})},
\end{align}$$ (A.2) where |$r$| is a constant required rate of return, |$D_t $| is the dividend observed at the end of the current period, and |$g^{\it ST}$| and |$g^{\it LT}$| are investors’ forecast of dividend growth in the next period and in every period thereafter respectively. Defining |$g^R$| as the actual constant growth rate in fundamentals, the next-period price of the short and the long-term assets and the next-period dividend are $$\begin{align}
P_{t+1}^{\it ST} &= 0,\\
\end{align}$$ (A.3) $$\begin{align}
P_{t+1}^{\it LT} &= P_t^{\it LT} (1+g^R),\\
\end{align}$$ (A.4) $$\begin{align}
D_{t+1} &= D_t (1+g^R).
\end{align}$$ (A.5)

Equation ( A.3 ) is a reminder that the ex-dividend price of the short-term asset is zero (i.e., no further cash flows are received after the dividend |$D_{t+1} $| is paid out). By contrast, Equation ( A.4 ) shows that following an observed growth rate of |$g^R$|⁠ , the ex-dividend price of the long-term asset is higher by a factor that is proportional to the growth in the most recent dividend from |$D_t$| to |$D_{t+1} $|⁠ , as in Equation ( A.5 ). One can then write the gross return of the short-term asset, |$R_{t+1}^{\it ST},$| and the long-term asset, |$R_{t+1}^{\it LT}$|⁠ , between times |$t$| and |$t + 1$|⁠ : $$\begin{align}
R_{t+1}^{\it ST} &= \frac{D_{t+1}} {P_t^{\it ST}} - \frac{(1+g^R)(1+r)}{(1+
g^{\it ST})},
\end{align}$$ (A.6) $$\begin{align}
R_{t+1}^{\it LT} &= \frac{P_{t+1}^{\it LT}+D_{t+1}} {P_t^{\it LT}} \nonumber\\[3pt]
&= \frac{D_t (1+g^R)}{\frac{D_t (1+g^{\it ST})}{(1+r)}+
\frac{1}{(1+r)}\frac{D_t (1+g^{\it ST})(1+g^{\it LT})}{(r - g^{\it LT})}}+(1+
g^R)\nonumber \\[3pt]
&= \frac{(1+g^R)(r - g^{\it LT})}{(1+g^{\it ST})}+(1+g^R) \nonumber\\[3pt]
&= \frac{(1+g^R)(1+r)}{(1+g^{\it ST})}+\frac{(1+g^R)(g^{\it ST} - g^{\it LT})}{(1+g^{\it ST})}.
\end{align}$$ (A.7)

Taking the expected difference between the return of the long- and the short-term asset over the next period, one obtains the following equation for the equity term premium at |$t + 1$|⁠ : $$\begin{align}
{\it ETP}_{t+1} = \mbox{E}\left[ {R_{t+1}^{\it LT} - R_{t+1}^{\it ST}} \right] =
\frac{(1+g^R)(g^{\it ST} - g^{\it LT})}{(1+g^{\it ST})}.
\end{align}$$ (A.8)

{\it STG}_{t+3}+{\it STG}_{t+4}} \right].\\& = \frac{1}{5}\left[ {4\phi _0+3\phi _0 \phi _1+2\phi _0
\phi _1^2+\phi _0 \phi _1^3+{\it STG}_t \left( {1+\phi _1+
\phi _1^2+\phi _1^3+\phi _1^4} \right)} \right]\nonumber
\end{align}$$ (A.11)

A.3 Construction of “rational” earnings forecasts, skewness, and uncertainty

A.3.1 Random forests methodology

We use a random forest methodology to generate earnings forecasts. Random forests are built off decision trees. Decision trees start with a training data set and look across predictors and split values to find a partition that minimizes the sum of squared errors in the two new regions.15The process is then repeated for the resulting regions, and this process continues until some stopping criteria is reached. Visually, we can present the first step of a decision tree as

Predictions from this decision tree equal the average value of the region to which the predictors assign an observation. For example, in the simple one-step tree above, if an observation in our test data set has a predictor value less than|$x$|⁠, we would generate a forecast equal to the average of outcomes in box 1.

Random forests build off decision trees by creating many trees and injecting randomness into how trees are constructed. First, each tree is grown to a bootstrap resampled data set. Second, to reduce the correlation across trees, the split variable is selected from a random subset of the predictors.16

We restrict our data set to S&P firms. We create a training data set based on outcomes from the past 5 years (⁠|$t - 5, t - 1$|⁠). Our test data set uses predictors from year|$t$|to make short-term and long-term earnings forecasts.

For our specific implementation, we want to predict earnings. A drawback of using random forests to predict earnings is that earnings forecasts will be bounded by the earnings outcome in the training data set. We, therefore, predict an economically important outcome—earnings scaled by total assets—and then unscale our predicted outcome by total assets at the time of prediction to generate an earnings prediction.17We predict scaled operating earnings from Compustat since the long-term growth variable is a forecast of growth in operating earnings (per WRDS), not necessarily total earnings.

We followvan Binsbergen, Han, and Lopez-Lira (2022)to select predictors for the forecasting model. Likevan Binsbergen, Han, and Lopez-Lira (2022), we categorize the variables into three groups. In total, we consider 82 forecasting variables.

A.3.1.1 Random forest information set

A. Firm fundamentals

We use the CRSP/Compustat merged data set to determine the most recently released value of operating earnings scaled by total assets (hereafter, scaled earnings). We assume the month of release is the month of the day that comes 90 days after the annual fiscal year-end. We determine the most recent growth rate by dividing scaled earnings by the lag value of scaled earnings.We use Compustat quarterly to determine the most recent year-over-year growth rate in sales (saleq). To determine whether the information has been released by the forecast date, we use the quarterly reporting date from Compustat (rdq). We require the information to have been released before the forecast month. If rdq is missing, we use the fiscal period end date (datadate) variable in Compustat. C. Analyst forecasts

We use the median forecast from I/B/E/S unadjusted detailAny missing (or infinite) values of predictors are replaced with the median value from the associated year-month. We merge analyst forecasts with other information using the IBES-CRSP link table from WRDS. We restrict our set of observations to those which have an IBES forecast.The training data set for making|$x$|-year forecasts includes these predictors and the associated future|$x$|-year scaled earnings outcome variable.We make forecasts of scaled earnings for future reporting periods. Specifically, we make forecasts of scaled earnings at the future annual earnings announcements. We then convert these into earnings forecasts by multiplying them by total assets. We sum earnings forecasts to create an aggregate earnings forecast. To construct a short-term (1-year) growth forecast from this method, we use a weighted-average of the forecast of this year’s total earnings and next year’s total earnings. The weights account for how long it has been since the most recent earnings announcement.19We scaled this sum by last year’s total earnings to create an earnings growth forecast. WRDS suggests that long-term growth forecasts refer to a period of between 3 and 5 years. Therefore, we follow the same method used to calculate 1-year forecasts to calculate 3-, 4-, and 5-year growth forecasts. The average of these three growth forecasts equals the long-term random forest growth forecast.

A.3.2 Construction of our skewness measure

We use the earnings prediction model from Hou, Van Dijk, and Zhang (2012) to predict different quantiles of future earnings. Specifically, we estimate the following model using quantile regressions over the past 5 years to generate the 5th, 50th, and 95th percentiles next-year’s earnings forecast: $$\begin{align}
\mbox{Earnings}_{i,t+1} &=
\mbox{Earnings}_{i,t}+\mbox{Dividends}_{i,t} + \mbox{Total
Assets}_{i,t} \nonumber\\
&\quad +\mbox{Dividend Dummy}_{i,t}+\mbox{Negative Earnings}_{i,t}+\nonumber\\
&\quad + \mbox{Accruals}_{i,t}+\varepsilon _{i,t}.\end{align}$$ (A.12)

Earnings are calculated as income before extraordinary items (Compustat item: ib) minus special items (Compustat item: spi). We use Compustat item dvc as our measure of dividends. The negative earnings and dividend dummy variables equal one if earnings are less than zero or dividends are greater than zero, respectively. Total assets come from the Compustat item “at.” Accruals are defined as the sum of the change in noncash working capital (Compustat items: (act – che) – (lct – dlc)), the change in net noncurrent operating assets (Compustat items: (at – act – ivao) – (lt – lct – dltt)), and the change in net financial assets (Compustat items: (ivst|$+$|ivao) – (dltt|$+$|dlc|$+$|pstk)). If any of these three components is missing, we replace its value with zero.

We generate forecasts for future years using the coefficients estimated from our estimation window and values from year|$t$|⁠. We conservatively assume a firm’s earnings announcement date occurs 90 days after the end of the fiscal year (Compustat item: datadate). We use an estimation window of 5 years to estimate the model. We require the earnings announcement date of the outcome variable to have occurred before year|$t$|for the data to be included in our estimation window. For example, when forecasting 5-year-ahead earnings, we consider data points with 5-year earnings outcome variables from years|$t-1$|to|$t-5$|⁠. This typically involves including regressors from years|$t- 6$|to|$t- 10$|⁠.

We estimate coefficients using quantile regressions and use these coefficients alongside year |$t$| data to generate earnings forecasts for the 5th, 50th, and 95th percentiles. This generates earnings forecasts at the firm-year level. We create forecasts at the firm-month level by forward-filling these forecasts until the next forecast is created or for the next 11 months. We aggregate these forecasts at the monthly level to get 5th percentile aggregate earnings forecasts, 50th percentile aggregate earnings forecasts, and 95th percentile aggregate earnings forecasts. We proxy for skewness as $$\begin{align}
\mbox{Skewness}_t = \left( {\frac{95\mbox{th Percentile Earnings Forecast}_t
\mbox{ $-$ 50th Percentile Earnings Forecast}_t} {\mbox{50th Percentile
Earnings Forecast}_t \mbox{ $-$ 5th Percentile Earnings Forecast}_t} }
\right)\!.
\end{align}$$ (A.13)

We are interested in the difference between long-horizon earnings skewness and short-horizon earnings skewness. Therefore, we calculate our horizon-differenced skewness measure as the difference between the normalized long-term skewness (based on 5-year earnings forecasts) and the normalized short-term earnings forecasts (based on 1-year earnings forecasts).

A.3.3 Construction of our uncertainty measure

Jurado, Ludvigson, and Ng (2015)define uncertainty at horizon|$h$|for variable|$y$|as|$U_t (h) = \sqrt {E[(y_{t+h} - E[y_{t+h} \vert I_t ])^2\vert I_t ]} $|⁠, where|$I_t $|represents information at time|$t$|⁠. We want to calculate earnings uncertainty at long and short horizons. We use our earnings forecasts from Section A.3.I to replace|$E[y_{t+h} \vert I_t ]$|⁠. We then repeat the earnings forecasting process described in that section to generate forecasts of the squared forecast error. This serves as a measure of firm-level uncertainty for|$h$|-period-ahead earnings forecasts. We generate a short-term measure of firm-level uncertainty by taking a weighted average of this period’s earnings uncertainty and two-period-ahead’s earnings uncertainty, where the weight on this period’s earnings uncertainty equals the fraction of a year until this period’s earnings announcement. The long-term measure is based on a weighted average of four-periods ahead uncertainty and five-period-ahead uncertainty. We create an aggregate measure of short(long)-term uncertainty by taking a market-value weighted measure of short- (long)-term uncertainty. We want a measure of uncertainty that looks at the difference in uncertainty between long and short-horizons. Therefore, our measure of uncertainty equals the difference between the normalized long-term uncertainty and the normalized short-term uncertainty measure.

Indeed, for our purposes, it might be more desirable to have decision trees that split based on the sum of absolute forecast error since analysts are judged on their mean absolute forecast error (Gu and Wu 2003; Basu and Markov 2004). However, we were not able to find a random forest R function that splits based on absolute forecast error. We were able to find a Python one, but implementation proves impossibly slow for large data sets. Therefore, we continue to use the common method of splitting based on the sum of squared errors, but we truncate the training data set at the 5 |$\%$| level of the outcome variables. This mitigates the impact of extreme observations and should make our results look more like a decision tree that splits based on the sum of absolute errors than a decision tree that splits based on the sum of squared errors.

We then select the five most important variables to always be included in the subset for variable selection alongside an additional five randomly selected predictors.

17 This is another difference from the methodology of van Binsbergen, Han, and Lopez-Lira (2022) . van Binsbergen, Han, and Lopez-Lira (2022) predict earnings per share (EPS), while we predict earnings scaled by total assets. The reason is that we are interested in not only short-term growth but also long-term growth. WRDS defines analysts’ forecasts of ltg (long-term growth) as “The long term growth rate represents an expected annual increase in operating earnings.” This suggests that ltg is a forecast of earnings growth rather than EPS growth. However, predicting earnings via a random forecasting model restricts our forecasts to the span of earnings within the training sample. Therefore, we need to find a scaling variable for the prediction model. Scaling by the number of shares would put more emphasis on high price firms, scaling by total assets puts more emphasis on firms with high profitability. We think high profitability firms are likely more economically important than high price firms, so we decide to scale by total assets. After generating a prediction for scaled earnings, we unscale our forecasts by total assets at the time of prediction to create an earnings forecast.

18 We also exclude ROA because it has already been constructed.

19 Specifically, we approximate the next annual announcement date by adding 1 year from the most recent earnings announcement. The fraction of a year that has passed since the last announcement constitutes our weight. We match observations with I/B/E/S with other data based on firm-month observations. Therefore, it is possible that the previous “announcement date” may postdate the forecast date. Since our “announcement date” is conservative—90 days (since 2002, the SEC requires the data to be released within 60 days)—we deem this not an issue and consider forecasts made in the same month before the “announcement date” to have a weight of 365 days on this year’s earnings.
